Title: Biosynthesis, Structural, and Functional Attributes of Tocopherols in Planta; Past, Present, and Future Perspectives
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; maize; potato; tobacco; rice; Brassica juncea; Chlamydomonas reinhardtii; Synechocystis
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Zea mays; Solanum tuberosum; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa; Brassica juncea; Chlamydomonas reinhardtii; Synechocystis sp. PCC 6803
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Tocopherol pathway manipulation is recommended to improve vitamin E nutritional quality and abiotic-stress tolerance, but deployment should consider whole-network effects and compensatory antioxidant systems. Gene stacking with core biosynthetic genes and regulators outside the core pathway may outperform single-gene engineering.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Tocopherol pathway genes collectively increase tocopherol/vitamin E accumulation and contribute to antioxidant protection, stress acclimation, and broader signaling-related functions in plants.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of mutant characterization, transgenic overexpression/RNAi lines, metabolite profiling, subcellular localization, and stress-phenotype analyses
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Senescence; chloroplast-to-chromoplast transition; seed development; drought; salinity; osmotic stress; methyl viologen; high light; low temperature
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Stress responsive; elevated during senescence, seed development, and plastid developmental transitions
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Ascorbate; glutathione; carotenoid pathway components; phytohormone-related pathways (JA; SA; ABA; ethylene)
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article; many conclusions are synthesized from prior studies rather than from new experiments in one focal species. The review emphasizes that genes outside the core pathway, including transcription factors, transporters, storage and turnover factors, likely shape tocopherol phenotypes.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Single-gene engineering may have unpredictable effects due to pathway integration, antioxidant compensation, and tissue-specific phenotypes; possible shifts in ascorbate/glutathione pools and other isoprenoid metabolites
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Mixed pathway enzymes and regulatory components
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Primarily plastids, especially chloroplast inner envelope, thylakoids, plastoglobules, amyloplasts, chromoplasts
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tocopherol biosynthesis is a plastid-localized pathway conserved in photosynthetic organisms. 2. Pathway perturbation alters vitamin E accumulation, stress tolerance, and antioxidant-network behavior. 3. Tocopherols likely function beyond antioxidation, influencing signaling, hormone interactions, and gene expression.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Tocopherols (vitamin E; α-, β-, γ-, δ-tocopherol)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplasts of green tissues; plastoglobules; thylakoids; inner chloroplast envelope; seeds in plastids/amyloplasts and sometimes cytoplasmic lipid bodies; fruits/tubers/petals plastids
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant defense, protection from photo-oxidative damage and lipid peroxidation, modulation of stress responses, contribution to human nutrition and health

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful precursor-supply target to increase total tocopherol by elevating homogentisate production; should be combined with downstream pathway capacity to avoid bottlenecks.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts p-hydroxyphenylpyruvate to homogentisate by dioxygenation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports homogentisate formation and thereby promotes tocopherol biosynthesis under development and stress conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification summarized from prior mutant and biochemical studies; pathway assignment in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Stress-responsive; expression reported to be regulated under environmental stress and by jasmonate-related signaling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also contributes precursor to plastoquinone synthesis. In Synechocystis ortholog is slr0090.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect related aromatic/isoprenoid pathways such as plastoquinone metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important precursor-supply step; not explicitly established as sole rate-limiting step in this review
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid-associated pathway context; enzyme summarized as plastid localized pathway component in plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HPPD/PDS1 converts HPP to HGA, providing the aromatic headgroup precursor for tocopherol synthesis. 2. Its expression is linked to stress and jasmonate-related responses. 3. Increased precursor supply through this step can influence tocopherol accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target because it catalyzes the first committed step of tocopherol biosynthesis. Silencing decreases tocopherol and salt tolerance; overexpression/stacking with downstream genes may raise vitamin E and stress resilience.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses homogentisate and phytyldiphosphate to form 2-methyl-6-phytylbenzoquinol (MPBQ/2M6PBQ).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes total tocopherol accumulation; reduced HPT causes lower tocopherol and increased salt sensitivity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis, RNAi silencing in tobacco, metabolite profiling, stress assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Committed step forming universal tocopherol intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; osmotic stress; methyl viologen; seed development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active in tissues synthesizing tocopherols, including photosynthetic tissues and developing seeds
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: VTE3; VTE1; VTE4; VTE5-linked phytol salvage pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis ortholog At2g18950; Synechocystis ortholog slr1736. The relative activities of VTE1 and VTE3 determine the metabolic fate of the VTE2 product.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing lowers tocopherol and impairs salt tolerance; altering precursor flux may affect other plastidic isoprenoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE2/HPT catalyzes the first committed step in tocopherol biosynthesis. 2. Lower HPT expression decreases total tocopherol and increases salt sensitivity. 3. Because it controls pathway entry, it is a strong target for vitamin E biofortification and stress-tolerance engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant defense, lipid protection, nutritional vitamin E source

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to redirect flux toward methylated tocopherol intermediates and may be stacked with VTE1/VTE4 to optimize isoform composition and total vitamin E.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates 2-methyl-6-phytylbenzoquinol and related prenylbenzoquinols to form dimethylated benzoquinol intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determines metabolic fate of VTE2 product and supports γ-/α-tocopherol branch formation; also links tocopherol and plastoquinone-related metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene characterization and biochemical functional studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for γ-tocopherol and α-tocopherol branch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Multifunctional enzyme that also methylates 2M6GGBQ and 2M6SBQ, linking tocopherol precursor metabolism with plastoquinol synthesis. Synechocystis ortholog sll0418.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux changes may influence plastoquinone metabolism due to multifunctionality
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-determining step; relative activity with VTE1 influences flux fate
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE3 methylates prenylbenzoquinol intermediates in the tocopherol pathway. 2. The relative activities of VTE3 and VTE1 determine the fate of the VTE2-derived intermediate. 3. VTE3 is multifunctional and also participates in plastoquinone-related metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant protection and nutritional vitamin E contribution

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Nicotiana tabacum; Zea mays
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for improving total tocopherol and abiotic stress tolerance. Overexpression improved salt tolerance in rice and tobacco; useful for stacking with VTE4 to raise α-tocopherol while maintaining pathway throughput.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes mono- and dimethylated prenylbenzoquinol intermediates to form the chromanol ring, producing δ- or γ-tocopherol depending on substrate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for tocopherol biosynthesis; deficiency reduces α- and γ-tocopherol and weakens salt resistance, whereas overexpression enhances stress tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (Arabidopsis vte1; maize sxd1/rice mutants), overexpression in rice and tobacco, metabolite profiling, stress assays, localization studies
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms tocopherol intermediates/products by chromanol-ring closure
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; drought/water stress; high light; low temperature; photooxidative stress
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Stress-responsive; associated with photosynthetic tissues and plastids
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: VTE2; VTE3; VTE4; antioxidant network with AA and GSH
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Localized to plastoglobules in chloroplasts. Overexpression can raise α-tocopherol levels indirectly through increased pathway flux. Maize mutant sxd1 is linked to tocopherol cyclase deficiency; Synechocystis ortholog slr1737.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can alter antioxidant-network balance; in VTE1 overexpressors AA and GSH may decline in some contexts
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Second most vital enzyme; important control step but not solely established as universal rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE1 catalyzes chromanol-ring formation, generating δ- and γ-tocopherol from prenylbenzoquinol intermediates. 2. vte1 deficiency lowers tocopherol content and impairs tolerance to salt and photooxidative stresses. 3. Overexpression in rice and tobacco improves salt tolerance and elevates tocopherol-related protection.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant defense, photoprotection, membrane protection, stress adaptation

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Brassica juncea
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major target for vitamin E quality improvement because it converts γ-tocopherol to nutritionally superior α-tocopherol. Overexpression is recommended to increase α/γ ratio and improve stress tolerance, but isoform-specific stress effects should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates γ-tocopherol to α-tocopherol and δ-tocopherol to β-tocopherol at carbon 5 of the chromanol ring.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls isoform composition and increases α-tocopherol content; overexpression enhances tolerance to salt and other abiotic stresses in some species, whereas γ-TMT transgenics may accumulate γ-tocopherol under certain contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (vte4), transgenic overexpression, RNAi silencing, metabolite profiling, stress assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Terminal determinant of α-tocopherol branch; converts immediate precursor to final target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; osmotic stress; methyl viologen; drought-related stress contexts
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Associated with tissues producing α-tocopherol, including leaves; relevant during stress adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Localized to inner chloroplast membrane. This enzyme defines conversion to α-tocopherol, the most bioactive vitamin E isoform. Synechocystis ortholog slr0089.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Isoform shifts may alter stress responses; some γ-Toc-accumulating transgenics showed high tolerance, indicating α-tocopherol is not universally the only protective isoform under all conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE4 catalyzes the final methylation converting γ-tocopherol to α-tocopherol. 2. It is central for improving vitamin E quality by increasing the α-tocopherol proportion. 3. Manipulation of VTE4/γ-TMT affects abiotic-stress responses and isoform-specific accumulation patterns.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and other plastid-containing tissues; seeds depending on species
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major vitamin E activity in human nutrition; antioxidant and stress protection in plants

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target to boost seed tocopherol by enhancing phytol recycling from chlorophyll breakdown; useful in seed-focused vitamin E biofortification strategies, potentially stacked with VTE2/VTE4.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates free phytol in the salvage pathway enabling reutilization toward phytyldiphosphate formation for tocopherol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports prenyl-moiety supply for tocopherol synthesis, particularly seed γ-tocopherol derived from recycled phytol during chlorophyll breakdown.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Arabidopsis mutant cloning and pathway characterization summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Relevant during chlorophyll degradation and in seeds
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Phytyl phosphate kinase (not specifically named in table); chlorophyll catabolic machinery
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Salvage/recycling step upstream of prenyl supply
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identification of VTE5 supported the view that free phytol liberated from chlorophyll degradation is recycled into tocopherol biosynthesis. Synechocystis ortholog Slr1562.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May couple tocopherol engineering to chlorophyll turnover and senescence physiology
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important precursor-recycling step; not established as general rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VTE5 encodes phytol kinase in the phytol salvage pathway. 2. It enables recycling of free phytol released from chlorophyll breakdown into tocopherol biosynthesis. 3. This salvage route is especially relevant for γ-tocopherol synthesis in seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Tocopherols, especially seed γ-tocopherol via recycled phytol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant protection and vitamin E nutritional quality

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Tocopherol precursor supply from tyrosine catabolism
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream target to increase homogentisate supply under stress-responsive engineering, especially in combination with HPPD and downstream VTE genes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports precursor supply for tocopherol biosynthesis and is transcriptionally linked to stress- and jasmonate-associated induction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior expression studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool upstream of HPPD
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a regulated gene encoding an upstream precursor-supply enzyme rather than listed in the core table.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect broader aromatic amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAT provides the immediate precursor HPP from tyrosine. 2. Its expression is noted as responsive to stress and jasmonate-linked regulation. 3. It likely contributes to tocopherol accumulation by modulating precursor availability.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant protection and vitamin E formation

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Indirect engineering node for stress-responsive tocopherol accumulation; manipulating ethylene signaling may alter vitamin E levels, but broad pleiotropy makes it better as a contextual regulator than a primary breeding target.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ethylene signaling influences tocopherol biosynthesis under environmental stress contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant study summarized in review showing altered tocopherol biosynthesis in an ethylene-signaling-defective Arabidopsis mutant
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking stress signaling to tocopherol biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water stress; salt stress; environmental stresses broadly
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Stress-signaling components broadly active; specific patterns not reported in review
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Name: ETR1; ERS1; ETR2; EIN4; ERS2; CTR1; EIN2; EIN3; ERF1
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Ethylene receptors and downstream signaling cascade components
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review discusses the ethylene pathway as an upstream signaling network affecting tocopherol metabolism, but does not identify one single decisive tocopherol regulator gene among these components.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Major pleiotropic effects on growth, development, and multiple stress responses expected if manipulated
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Ethylene receptors; Raf-like kinase CTR1; membrane signal transducer EIN2; TFs EIN3 and ERF1
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Signal transduction leading to transcriptional regulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Hormone-response signaling components and transcription factors
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Alters expression of tocopherol biosynthesis genes under stress, direction not fully resolved in the review
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mixed: membrane receptors, cytosolic kinase, nuclear TFs
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ethylene signaling is implicated as an upstream regulator of tocopherol biosynthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. A mutant defective in ethylene signaling showed altered tocopherol biosynthesis. 3. This supports a broader hormone-signaling network controlling tocopherol accumulation under stress.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ethylene; environmental stresses including drought and salt

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JA pathway can be leveraged to induce upstream precursor genes for stress-triggered tocopherol accumulation, but broad defense-growth tradeoffs require caution.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Jasmonic acid acts as an upstream signal associated with induction of tocopherol biosynthetic precursor genes and stress-linked tocopherol accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Correlative physiological and expression evidence summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent hormonal regulator of tocopherol accumulation under abiotic stress
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not applicable for hormone itself; pathway active under stress
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward coupling via lipid peroxidation and stress response, not directly resolved
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Precursor supply and stress-responsive tocopherol biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review explicitly states JA regulates expression of genes encoding TAT and HPPD, but specific TF genes are not named.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Defense activation and growth penalties possible
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes expression of TAT and HPPD-related genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JA accumulates from lipid peroxidation and mediates stress responses. 2. It regulates expression of upstream tocopherol biosynthetic genes such as TAT and HPPD. 3. This links oxidative stress, hormone signaling, and vitamin E biosynthesis.
